prompt: You spend more time thinking about what users see than where data lives.
You have shipped something that runs on an end user's device, not a server.
Your craft lives primarily in the browser rather than in native app stores.
You reach for a design system or component library before writing raw CSS.
Bundle size is something you actively track and optimise on each pull request.
Accessibility audits and ARIA semantics regularly show up in your code reviews.
You would rather refine a pixel-perfect component than profile a render cycle.
You regularly open Xcode or Android Studio to debug platform-specific behaviour.
You own the database schema and the deployment pipeline on top of the UI.
Push notification latency and offline sync keep you up at night more than server costs.
You prefer writing Swift or Kotlin over picking a shared cross-platform abstraction.
You maintain a published component library that other teams actually consume.
Design tokens and theming are a first-class concern in your component architecture.
You regularly write Storybook stories before the feature ticket is complete.
You have an opinion on when to use web components versus a framework component.
You profile Largest Contentful Paint before a feature ships to production.
You have replaced JavaScript-heavy solutions with WebAssembly for a real project.
Hydration strategies and streaming SSR are topics you follow closely and enjoy debating.
You treat Core Web Vitals regressions as blocking bugs, not nice-to-fix issues.
You write SwiftUI or Jetpack Compose rather than managing layout in XML or UIKit.
App Store review cycles and deployment profiles are part of your weekly workflow.
You measure app quality in ANR rates and cold-start times, not just crash rates.
You have integrated platform-specific APIs like ARKit, HealthKit, or CameraX directly.
You write both the REST or GraphQL API and the React component that consumes it.
You reach for Supabase or a managed BaaS to skip boilerplate and ship faster.
You manage database migrations and schema evolution as carefully as application code.
You deploy and own the entire feature from data model to URL in production.
You have written a custom CSS-in-JS or token-to-CSS pipeline for a project.
You test interactive component behaviour with user-event and RTL before shipping.
Your ideal PR contains a Storybook link, a design-token diff, and zero layout regressions.
You have used a performance budget tool to gate merges on a CI pipeline.
You enjoy reading browser engine release notes for new rendering capabilities.
You can explain the difference between TTFB, FCP, and LCP to a non-technical stakeholder.
You have debugged a memory leak in a native module on both iOS and Android.
You deliberately choose platform-idiomatic gestures over convenient generic cross-platform shortcuts.
You keep separate Swift and Kotlin codebases rather than one shared layer for a reason.
You have wired an auth provider, a database, and a deployment pipeline in a single weekend.
You choose technologies by how quickly they let you validate a product hypothesis.
You feel equally at home writing a migration script and a React hook on the same day.
Do you work on infrastructure or platform systems rather than end-user features?
Is keeping systems running reliably a core part of your role?
Do you spend more time provisioning cloud resources than hardening or tooling them?
Do you manage Kubernetes clusters in production for other engineering teams?
Is infrastructure-as-code your primary approach to provisioning and managing cloud resources?
Do you define SLOs and own the on-call rotation for your services?
Do you think more about blast radius and change management than incident response?
Do you review code for security vulnerabilities as part of your regular workflow?
Have you written or maintained a CLI tool used by other developers?
Do you model threats or run penetration tests against your own systems?
Does improving developer experience and build speed matter more than threat hunting?
Do you use Terraform or OpenTofu to provision multi-cloud environments at scale?
Have you designed a golden-path platform that internal teams adopt without coercion?
Do you routinely write runbooks and post-mortems that teams actually reference later?
Is reducing toil and eliminating manual cloud operations your main engineering goal?
Do you instrument services with distributed tracing to debug latency across dependencies?
Have you authored SLO budgets that changed how engineers prioritize reliability work?
Do you treat observability as a first-class engineering discipline, not an afterthought?
Is writing blameless post-mortems and improving incident processes part of your job?
Do you integrate security scanning into CI/CD pipelines before code reaches production?
Have you dealt with a supply-chain attack or managed an SBOM for a production service?
Do you own secrets management and access control policies across your organisation?
Is threat modelling something you embed into sprint planning rather than a pre-launch audit?
Do you build or maintain tools that speed up other engineers' daily development loops?
Have you designed or significantly improved a CI/CD pipeline used by multiple teams?
Do you reach for Rust or Go when building performance-sensitive developer tooling?
Do you measure your impact in keystrokes or minutes saved per engineer per week?
Do you treat cloud cost optimisation as seriously as you treat uptime and deployment speed?
Have you migrated a workload between cloud providers and lived to tell the story?
Is your ultimate goal a self-service cloud platform that teams deploy to without a ticket?
Do you track error budgets and use them to negotiate feature-vs-reliability trade-offs?
Do you prefer Prometheus and Grafana over vendor-managed monitoring dashboards?
Do you believe on-call pain is a design failure that good observability prevents?
Do you track zero-days and CVEs the way other engineers track framework release notes?
Have you used cryptography libraries or designed an authentication flow from scratch?
Do you believe every sprint review should include a security sign-off, not just a QA pass?
Do you obsess over build times and test feedback loops the way others obsess over latency?
Have you contributed to or authored an open-source developer tool with active users?
Is your north star a world where no engineer ever waits more than five seconds for feedback?
Do you spend meaningful time working with data-intensive systems or AI models?
Does your work produce outputs that other systems or people consume downstream?
Do you train, fine-tune, or evaluate AI and machine-learning models?
Do you regularly write Python to preprocess datasets or engineer features?
Have you deployed a model to serve predictions in a live production environment?
Do you read papers or track benchmarks to stay ahead on model architecture advances?
Is GPU utilisation and training efficiency a regular concern in your day-to-day work?
Do you build or maintain pipelines that move data between systems at scale?
Is SQL your primary language for exploring, transforming, or modelling data?
Do you care deeply about pipeline reliability, SLAs, and data-quality guarantees?
Do you orchestrate batch or streaming jobs using tools like Airflow, Spark, or Flink?
Do you work with large neural-network models trained on GPU clusters?
Have you built or operated an MLOps platform for model versioning and serving?
Is debugging training instability, loss curves, or gradient issues part of your routine?
Do you evaluate models using held-out test sets, benchmarks, or red-teaming?
Do you build products or features that call LLM APIs as their core component?
Have you implemented a retrieval-augmented generation system in a real codebase?
Do you iterate on prompts and system instructions as a core part of your development loop?
Are you shipping end-user AI features rather than working on the underlying model itself?
Do you design the storage layer for a data lake, lakehouse, or warehouse architecture?
Does your work involve streaming data with tools like Kafka, Flink, or Kinesis?
Have you dealt with CDC, schema evolution, or exactly-once delivery guarantees?
Do you think about backfill strategies and pipeline idempotency when designing jobs?
Do you write dbt models or define metric layers for business intelligence consumption?
Is your primary deliverable a dashboard, report, or self-service analytics tool?
Do stakeholders come to you to define or debate what a metric should actually measure?
Do you use Python alongside SQL to run statistical or predictive analysis?
Do you prefer low-level control over model internals over high-level abstractions?
Have you worked with distributed training across multiple GPUs or nodes?
Is reproducibility and experiment tracking a non-negotiable part of how you work?
Do you evaluate and choose between foundation models from multiple providers?
Have you wired tool use, function calling, or agents into an AI-powered product?
Do you obsess over latency and cost per token as much as output quality?
Do you own the contract between data producers and downstream consumers?
Have you migrated data systems across storage formats like Parquet, Iceberg, or Delta?
Does a data outage or late pipeline feel like a production incident to you?
Do you maintain a canonical set of metric definitions that the whole business relies on?
Do you use DuckDB, Snowflake, or BigQuery as a daily driver for analysis?
Do you believe a clean data model is more valuable than a flashy dashboard?
Do you work primarily outside mainstream web and cloud domains?
Does your work involve hardware, teams, or building a company from scratch?
Are you more drawn to interactive or physical computing than org and business concerns?
Have you written code that renders frames, polygons, or shader pipelines?
Do you care deeply about real-time performance and cycle-accurate timing?
Have you shipped something that runs on a game engine or custom render loop?
Is your target environment a screen with a player, not a chip with a sensor?
Do you spend significant time on hiring, mentoring, or shaping engineering culture?
Have you been on the hook for product strategy or business model decisions?
Does your calendar look more like sprint planning than investor meetings?
Is your primary identity growing a team rather than growing a company?
Do you write or profile GPU shader code as part of regular work?
Have you shipped a game or interactive experience to end users or a store?
Does your engine of choice have a scene graph and a physics engine built in?
Is frame budget and draw-call optimization a genuine daily concern for you?
Do you program microcontrollers or write code that talks directly to hardware registers?
Does your system ever run without a full OS kernel underneath it?
Have you integrated physical sensors, actuators, or radio protocols in a project?
Do you think about memory layout and stack size when every kilobyte counts?
Do you set engineering-wide standards like on-call rotation, code review norms, or tech radar?
Have you managed other managers or led multiple engineering squads at once?
Is your success metric the output of your team rather than your own commits?
Do you spend time on org design, headcount planning, or performance frameworks?
Have you raised external funding or pitched your company to investors?
Do you personally write production code while also owning business outcomes?
Have you made a build-vs-buy or tech-stack decision based on runway constraints?
Is finding product-market fit more urgent to you than technical elegance?
Do you target multiple platforms like PC, console, and mobile simultaneously?
Does procedural generation, AI behaviour trees, or simulation logic appear in your codebase?
Would you describe yourself as a game developer first and a software engineer second?
Do you work with RTOS scheduling, interrupt handlers, or power management routines?
Is robotics, industrial automation, or wearables in your professional scope?
Do you debug issues with an oscilloscope or logic analyser rather than a browser console?
Do you regularly run one-on-ones to unblock engineers rather than write features yourself?
Does managing technical debt and architectural decisions across teams fall on you?
Would you rather grow ten engineers than write the most elegant code yourself?
Have you shipped a product that paid customers actually rely on every day?
Do you think about CAC, LTV, or churn as often as you think about system architecture?
Would you describe yourself as a founder who can code, not an engineer who has a side project?
